_meta.description: English aggregation and statistics terms dictionary for the Excel Python Agent.

terms.description: The sum of values divided by the count.
The middle value when data is sorted in ascending order.
The value that appears most frequently in a dataset.
The nth root of the product of n values; used for growth rates and ratios.
The reciprocal of the arithmetic mean of reciprocals; used for rates and ratios.
An average where each value is multiplied by a weight reflecting its importance.
The mean calculated after removing a percentage of extreme values from both ends.
The average of the maximum and minimum values in a dataset.
The difference between the maximum and minimum values.
The average of squared deviations from the mean; measures data spread.
The square root of variance; measures the average distance from the mean.
The range between the 25th and 75th percentiles; robust measure of spread.
The average of absolute deviations from the mean.
The ratio of standard deviation to the mean, expressed as a percentage.
The standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the mean.
The smallest value in a dataset.
The largest value in a dataset.
A general term for how much values differ from each other and the center.
The peak-to-trough distance in oscillating data; synonym for range in some contexts.
The difference between an observed value and a reference value such as the mean.
A value below which a given percentage of observations fall.
Values that divide data into four equal parts.
The value below which 25% of the data falls.
The value below which 75% of the data falls.
The value below which 5% of the data falls; lower tail boundary.
The value below which 10% of the data falls.
The value below which 25% of the data falls; equivalent to Q1.
The value below which 50% of the data falls; equivalent to the median.
The value below which 75% of the data falls; equivalent to Q3.
The value below which 90% of the data falls.
The value below which 95% of the data falls; upper tail boundary.
The value below which 99% of the data falls; extreme upper tail.
Values that divide data into ten equal parts.
A general term for cut points dividing a probability distribution into equal intervals.
A measure of asymmetry of the probability distribution around the mean.
A measure of the heaviness of tails relative to a normal distribution.
A symmetric probability distribution defined by its mean and standard deviation.
The number of standard deviations a data point is from the mean.
A data point significantly different from other observations, often beyond 1.5x IQR.
The characteristic shape of a normal distribution; symmetric and unimodal.
A distribution with tails heavier than a normal distribution, indicating more extreme events.
A distribution with two distinct peaks, suggesting two underlying groups.
The total obtained by adding all values together.
The number of observations or non-missing values in a dataset.
The number of unique values in a dataset.
A cumulative sum that updates as each new value is added.
The running total of values up to the current position.
Partitioning data into groups based on one or more keys, then aggregating within each group.
A total for a subset or section of data, before the grand total.
The total of all subtotals; the overall aggregate value.
An aggregation that calculates subtotals at multiple hierarchy levels.
A table that restructures data by turning unique values into columns for multi-dimensional aggregation.
A frequency table of two or more categorical variables showing joint distributions.
A running count of occurrences, typically for categorical data.
To progressively gather and combine values over successive steps.
A function that performs a calculation across a set of rows related to the current row.
The sum of values within a sliding window that moves through the dataset.
The rate at which a value increases over a period, expressed as a fraction or percentage.
The annualized growth rate that smooths out volatility over multiple periods.
The proportional change between two values expressed as a percentage.
The change in a metric compared to the same period in the prior year.
The change in a metric compared to the immediately preceding month.
The change in a metric compared to the immediately preceding quarter.
The absolute difference between two values.
The actual numerical difference between two values without normalization.
The change between two values expressed as a fraction of the original value.
The rate of change of a value with respect to time.
The quantitative relation between two amounts showing their relative sizes.
A part expressed as a fraction of the whole.
A value normalized to a base period, typically expressed as base 100.
A standard or reference point against which performance is measured.
An initial measurement or starting value used for comparison.
A value expressed in terms of another reference value.
The percentage that a part represents of the whole.
A factor assigned to a value indicating its relative importance in an aggregation.
A measure of the linear relationship between two variables, ranging from -1 to 1.
The proportion of variance in the dependent variable explained by the independent variable.
A statistical method for modeling the relationship between dependent and independent variables.
The rate of change of the dependent variable per unit change in the independent variable.
The expected value of the dependent variable when the independent variable is zero.
A numerical value representing the relationship strength between a predictor and the response.
The probability of observing results at least as extreme as the actual results, assuming the null hypothesis is true.
Whether an observed effect is unlikely to have occurred by chance, typically at alpha = 0.05.
A hypothesis test that compares the means of one or two groups using the t-distribution.
A test of independence or goodness-of-fit for categorical data.
A test comparing means across three or more groups to determine if at least one differs.
A test comparing two variances to determine if they are significantly different.
Replacing extreme values with the nearest non-extreme values to reduce the impact of outliers.
Removing a percentage of extreme values from both ends of a distribution before computing statistics.
A resampling technique that repeatedly draws samples with replacement to estimate statistics.

terms.numpyMethod: np.array([statistic(np.delete(x, i)) for i in range(len(x))])
terms.description: A resampling technique that systematically leaves out one observation at a time.
A robust estimator that combines least squares for small residuals and absolute value for large residuals.
The maximum proportion of arbitrarily corrupted data an estimator can handle before giving incorrect results.
A statistic that is not strongly influenced by extreme values or outliers.
Statistical methods that do not assume a specific probability distribution for the data.
The average of values within a sliding window that moves through a time series.
A smoothing technique that gives exponentially decreasing weights to older observations.
A value from a previous time period, used for calculating changes and autocorrelation.
A value from a future time period, used for forward-looking calculations.
Removing periodic seasonal effects from time series data to reveal underlying trends.
Removing the long-term trend component from time series data.
The correlation of a time series with a delayed copy of itself.
terms.formula: r_k = Σ(x_t - μ)(x_{t-k} - μ) / Σ(x_t - μ)²

terms.description: A property of a time series whose statistical properties do not change over time.
terms.formula: E[x_t] = μ, Var(x_t) = σ² for all t

terms.description: The long-term directional movement in a time series, ignoring short-term fluctuations.
An estimate of future values based on historical patterns and trends.
A measure of how likely an event is to occur, ranging from 0 to 1.
The probability of the observed data given a set of parameter values.
A range of values within which the true population parameter is expected to fall at a given confidence level.
A range of values within which a future individual observation is expected to fall.
A Bayesian interval within which a parameter value falls with a specified probability given the observed data.
The probability distribution representing beliefs about a parameter before observing data.
The probability distribution of a parameter after combining the prior with observed data via Bayes' theorem.
A statistical approach that updates probability estimates as new evidence is observed using Bayes' theorem.
The ordinal position of a value when all values are sorted.
The percentage of values in a dataset that are equal to or less than a given value.
A ranking method where tied values receive the same rank and the next rank is not skipped.
A unique sequential integer assigned to each row in a result set.
Divides an ordered dataset into n approximately equal groups and assigns a group number.
The n highest values in a dataset.
The n lowest values in a dataset.
A ranking where each item receives a distinct rank, with ties broken by order of appearance.
A ranking where tied values receive the average of the ranks they would have occupied.
